title: Debt Charity Demands End to Prison Threats for Unpaid Council Tax
description: StepChange calls on local councils to stop threatening imprisonment for missed council tax payments, calling the practice counterproductive.
lede: Debt charity StepChange is urging local authorities to stop threatening residents with prison time over unpaid council tax. Ahead of widespread tax hikes this April, the charity exposed that one in 20 councils in England and Wales cite potential imprisonment in their very first letter following a single missed payment.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: A new report from StepChange, titled "Clear, Fair, Understandable," revealed stark differences in how local authorities pursue unpaid council tax. Freedom of Information requests showed that 36% of councils explicitly reference debt recovery or enforcement action in their initial missed-payment letter, a figure that climbs to 50% by the final notice.

Most aggressively, 5% of councils immediately raise the prospect of imprisonment in their first communication. The charity described this messaging as "unsympathetic" and "oversimplified," warning that it often frightens vulnerable people into ignoring the letters entirely rather than seeking help.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: The threat of prison is largely disconnected from reality. According to StepChange, only four people have actually been imprisoned for failing to pay council tax since 2020, alongside 143 suspended sentences. The ultimate sanction only applies in cases of "wilful refusal" or "culpable neglect" and is legally restricted to England, excluding Wales and Scotland.

Peter Tutton, director of policy at StepChange, labeled the imprisonment sanction as "outdated" and demanded its removal. The charity emphasized that heavy-handed tactics penalize those who cannot pay, rather than those who simply refuse to pay, driving individuals deeper into problem debt and extreme hardship, such as foregoing food to cover bills.

sections.heading: The Bigger Picture
paragraphs: Local councils are walking a tightrope between supporting struggling residents and securing the funding required to operate. The Local Government Association (LGA), representing councils across England and Wales, defended the collections process. A spokesperson stressed that councils have a legal duty to collect taxes to prevent cuts to essential services, including elderly care, child protection, and road maintenance.

The LGA maintained that authorities strive to recover unpaid taxes sympathetically and offer support to households at risk of financial exclusion. However, the impending April tax increases threaten to push more households into arrears, amplifying the tension between local government funding gaps and the escalating cost-of-living crisis.

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: StepChange is pushing for immediate reforms to collection practices, advocating for an end to the imprisonment sanction and the adoption of more sensitive messaging. The charity highlighted positive examples, such as councils replacing red-font threats with supportive language offering to help residents get back on track.

As the April tax hikes take effect, scrutiny on local authority collection tactics will intensify. Residents struggling to meet their obligations are urged by both the LGA and debt charities to contact their councils immediately to arrange support and avoid aggressive enforcement action.
